en: Financial transaction failures and billing disputes where the credit card was declined, the online payment link timed out, or the bank transfer never arrived. Guests report being double-charged, seeing unauthorized holds on their statement, or waiting for a pending refund that has not cleared. Typical triggers: "my card got declined", "you charged me twice", "the payment link is broken", "where is my refund", "I need an invoice for tax purposes".

After departure, a former occupant realizes they left personal belongings behind — a phone charger plugged into the bedroom wall, a laptop on the desk, clothing in the wardrobe, or groceries still in the refrigerator. They urgently ask someone to check the unit and arrange courier shipping or a pickup time. Typical triggers: "I forgot my charger", "we left a suitcase behind", "can someone look for my passport", "there's food in the fridge we need back".

Once the stay has ended, a former occupant disputes the security deposit deduction or questions a surprise charge that appeared on their credit card statement days after departure. They challenge a damage claim, demand proof of the alleged damage, or ask when the held deposit funds will be released. Typical triggers: "when do I get my deposit back", "why was I charged extra after leaving", "I dispute the damage fee", "show me photos of the supposed damage".

A former occupant contacts the host after their trip to share retrospective feedback, express lingering dissatisfaction about their overall experience, or discuss the review they intend to publish. They reflect on accumulated issues from their stay and seek acknowledgment or a goodwill gesture now that they have departed. Typical triggers: "I want to give formal feedback about my stay", "the experience was disappointing overall", "I am writing a review and wanted to discuss", "I expected better and want this documented".

Coordinating the physical journey to reach the building before moving in. Guests request the Google Maps pin, driving directions from the highway, or the nearest metro station. They ask about airport shuttle services, Uber pickup zones, taxi fare estimates, or whether on-site vehicle parking is available in the garage. They may also share their flight number and estimated landing time so the host knows when to expect them. Typical triggers: "send me the location pin", "how do I get there from the airport", "is there parking in the building", "what's the nearest landmark", "our flight lands at 10pm".

Haggling over the nightly tariff because the traveler finds it steep and wants a markdown. They explicitly ask for a discount, a coupon code, or a reduced rate because their budget is tight. Typical triggers: "can you lower the price", "that's too expensive for us", "any discount available", "what's the cheapest you can do", "we're on a tight budget".

Benchmarking the listed tariff against competitor platforms or rival listings and questioning whether the rate is fair. The traveler mentions spotting a lower figure on Airbnb, Booking.com, or a nearby competing property and wants the host to match or beat it. Typical triggers: "I saw it cheaper on Airbnb", "Booking.com shows a lower rate", "can you price-match", "the place next door charges less", "is that the final price or negotiable".

Requesting volume-based or corporate rate reductions for multi-week stays, group reservations, or business travel arrangements. The traveler asks about weekly bundles, loyalty rebates, or whether an employer-negotiated corporate tariff applies. Typical triggers: "what's the weekly rate", "do you offer a corporate rate", "we're booking for a team of six", "any bulk discount for 10 nights", "my company will pay — do you do business rates".

Factual reference data about the building and its surroundings — the exact street address, which floor number the unit occupies, whether the building has a lift, proximity to the nearest supermarket or pharmacy, and neighborhood safety. Guests also ask about house rules such as the quiet hours policy, smoking restrictions, maximum occupancy, and pet regulations. Typical triggers: "what's the exact address", "which floor is it on", "is there an elevator", "how far is the nearest grocery store", "are pets allowed", "what are the house rules", "is the neighborhood safe".

Visual and spatial overview of how the living space looks and feels — bedroom count, floor plan layout, square meters, decor style, view from windows, balcony or terrace, pool access, security details. Typical triggers: "how many bedrooms", "describe the place", "what does the apartment look like", "is there a pool", "is there a balcony", "how big is the apartment", "is there parking".

Requesting soft furnishing items related to bedding and linens. The occupant needs extra pillows, a thicker duvet, additional bath towels, fresh fitted sheets, or a portable baby cot. Typical triggers: "we need more towels", "can we get an extra pillow", "the blanket is too thin", "do you have a crib for an infant", "please bring clean bed linens".

Asking about household appliances, gadgets, and electronic devices available inside the unit. The occupant wants to know if there is an iron and ironing board, a hairdryer, a blender, an espresso machine, a microwave, a dishwasher, or a front-load washer. They also request hangers, a phone charger, or a specific kitchen gadget they expected to find. Typical triggers: "is there an iron", "where is the hairdryer", "does the kitchen have a blender", "do you have a phone charger I can borrow", "how do I use the washing machine".

Requesting consumable supplies, toiletries, and kitchenware provisions. The occupant asks for dish soap, sponges, coffee capsules, cooking oil, children's plates and sippy cups, bathroom toiletries like shampoo and conditioner, or trash bags. Typical triggers: "we ran out of dish soap", "can you send coffee pods", "do you have kids' plates", "there's no shampoo", "we need more garbage bags".

Withdrawing an existing reservation because plans fell through. The traveler's trip was called off due to a medical emergency, visa denial, family situation, or schedule conflict, and they want to know how to cancel, what the cancellation penalty is, the deadline for a full refund, and whether partial credit is available if they are past the free-cancellation window. Typical triggers: "I need to cancel my reservation", "something came up and we can't make it", "what's your cancellation policy", "can I still get a full refund if I cancel now", "our trip fell through".

Verifying that a completed reservation was received, recorded, and processed in the system. The traveler already submitted a booking and now wants reassurance it went through — they share their confirmation code, ask whether the host can see the reservation on their end, or request a summary of the reserved dates and unit assignment. Typical triggers: "is my booking confirmed", "I booked last week but haven't heard back", "can you verify my reservation number", "did my reservation go through", "please confirm the dates I reserved".

Checking date availability before making any reservation. The prospective traveler has no existing booking and wants to know which units are free for their desired travel window. They specify arrival and departure dates and ask whether anything is open tonight, next weekend, or over a holiday period. Typical triggers: "is anything available from June 5 to June 10", "do you have a unit free tonight", "are you fully booked next weekend", "checking availability for four nights in July".

Pre-booking cost inquiry to understand the total expense before committing. The prospective traveler asks for a rate quote for specific dates, wants to know what the nightly tariff includes (cleaning fee, taxes), or requests a total-cost breakdown for their desired stay duration. Typical triggers: "how much would it cost for 5 nights in August", "what does the nightly rate include", "can you send me a price quote", "what's the total for those dates including fees".

Pre-booking suitability questions to decide if the listing matches their needs. The prospective traveler asks about the number of sleeping areas, whether it can accommodate a family of five, if there is a pool or fitness center on-site, or how close the building is to the convention center or beach. Typical triggers: "is this suitable for a family with kids", "how many people can it sleep", "is there a pool on the premises", "how close is it to the expo center", "does it have two separate sleeping areas".

Altering the details of an already-confirmed reservation. The occupant wants to extend by adding extra nights at the end, shorten by removing nights from their itinerary, shift the entire date range forward or backward, upgrade to a larger unit, or update the headcount on the reservation after their travel party changed. Typical triggers: "can I add two more nights", "I want to shorten my stay", "can we switch the dates to next week instead", "is it possible to move to a bigger unit", "we now have an extra person joining".

Requesting housekeeping, maid service, or a deep-clean session for the unit during the stay. The occupant wants someone to mop the tile floors, vacuum the carpets, scrub the bathroom, change the bed linens, or wipe down kitchen surfaces. They may ask about scheduling recurring tidy-up visits every few days or demand an urgent cleanup because the space feels dusty or grimy. Typical triggers: "can you send a cleaner", "how much does housekeeping cost", "the place needs mopping", "we want cleaning every three days", "it's getting dusty in here".

Expressing shock and disgust at the physical state of the unit upon arrival or during the stay. The occupant finds stained mattresses, hair in the bathtub, grease on kitchen counters, or discovers the space looks nothing like the listing photos. Their tone is angry and accusatory. Typical triggers: "this place is filthy", "the photos are completely misleading", "there are stains on the sheets", "I'm disgusted by the condition", "this is false advertising".

Voicing outrage over noise disturbances, disruptive neighbors, or unbearable environmental conditions inside the building. The occupant reports neighbors partying until dawn, jackhammer construction at 7am, hallway odors seeping under the door, or temperatures that make the unit unlivable despite the HVAC running. Their language conveys sleep deprivation and mounting frustration. Typical triggers: "the neighbors are partying every night", "construction wakes us up at dawn", "there is a terrible smell in the corridor", "we can't sleep because of the noise", "the heat is unbearable".

Demanding to speak with a manager, threatening a negative public review, or declaring the entire experience unacceptable. The occupant feels ignored by the host, believes they were misled about what they paid for, or insists that the level of service is far below expectations. Their language is emotionally charged with words like unacceptable, terrible, worst, furious, and rip-off. Typical triggers: "I want to talk to a manager right now", "I'm leaving a one-star review", "this is the worst experience I've ever had", "nobody is helping us", "I feel completely ripped off".

Requesting access to the unit before the standard 3 PM move-in hour because the traveler's inbound flight lands at dawn or midmorning. They want to drop off suitcases, shower after a red-eye, or settle in early so young children can nap. The conversation centers on morning arrival logistics — 6 AM, 8 AM, 10 AM — and whether the unit will be ready ahead of schedule. Typical triggers: "our flight lands at 6 AM, can we come straight there", "is early access possible", "we need to drop bags before noon", "can we get in at 10 instead of 3", "the kids need to nap after the flight".

Requesting permission to remain in the unit past the standard 11 AM vacate hour on the final day because the traveler's outbound flight departs in the afternoon or evening. They want to extend their last morning by a few hours — leaving at 2 PM, 3 PM, or even 6 PM — to avoid waiting at the airport with luggage. The conversation centers on departure-day timing and how long they can stay beyond the vacate deadline. Typical triggers: "our flight is at 9 PM, can we stay until 6", "can we leave at 3 instead of 11", "we don't want to sit at the airport all day", "is late departure possible", "what time do we have to be out".

Inquiring about multi-month tenancy arrangements for stays measured in weeks or months rather than nights. The prospective tenant is relocating for a work assignment, undergoing medical treatment in the city, or needs transitional housing between permanent leases. They ask about the monthly rental rate, whether utilities are bundled, lease terms for 60- or 90-day stays, and corporate housing contracts. Typical triggers: "what's the monthly rate", "I'm relocating for work and need a place for three months", "do you offer long-term leases", "I need housing for a 60-day assignment", "are utilities included in the monthly price".

Reporting something broken, malfunctioning, or in need of urgent repair inside the unit. The occupant describes an air-conditioning compressor that stopped cooling, a washing machine drum that won't spin, a refrigerator that's warm inside, a toilet that overflows when flushed, water dripping from the ceiling, no hot water from the taps, flickering or dead light fixtures, tripped circuit breakers, or pest sightings such as cockroaches and ants. Mold growth, foul drain odors, and jammed window locks also fall here. Typical triggers: "the AC isn't working", "the toilet is clogged", "there's a water leak under the sink", "we have no hot water", "I saw cockroaches in the kitchen", "the lights keep flickering".

Requesting an in-person site visit, walkthrough, or inspection of the unit before committing to a reservation. The prospective tenant wants to physically tour the space, schedule a viewing appointment at a convenient time, or ask if they can bring a friend or partner along to see the unit together. They may also ask for a live video call tour, request additional photographs of specific rooms, or inquire about permission to film content or conduct a professional photoshoot on the premises. Typical triggers: "can I come see the place first", "I'd like to schedule a viewing", "can we do a video tour", "I want to visit before deciding", "can I bring someone along to see it", "is filming allowed inside".

Asking whether external visitors, friends, relatives, or colleagues are permitted to enter the unit. The occupant wants a friend to come over for dinner, a sibling to stay overnight, a work colleague to drop off documents, or family members to visit during the weekend. They may push back on restrictions, arguing the visit will be brief or the person is a close relative. Typical triggers: "can my friend come over for dinner", "is it okay if my sister stays the night", "a colleague needs to drop something off", "my parents want to visit on Saturday", "what's your visitor policy", "are overnight visitors allowed".

Requesting WiFi network credentials, digital lock PIN codes, or building entry instructions. The occupant needs the wireless network name and password to get online, the numeric keypad code for the front door or unit door, or step-by-step instructions for the smart lock. They also report connectivity failures — the internet is slow, the WiFi keeps dropping, or the password is rejected — and lockout emergencies where the keypad code fails and they are stranded outside. Typical triggers: "what's the WiFi password", "the door code isn't working", "I'm locked out", "how do I connect to the internet", "the WiFi keeps disconnecting", "which network do I join".
